stem:
A 32-year-old G1P0 at 40 weeks has been pushing for 30 minutes. The head delivers but retracts firmly against the perineum and external rotation fails. McRoberts hyperflexion has been performed and suprapubic pressure applied for 30 seconds without delivery. Estimated foetal weight at 38 weeks was 4.0 kg. The mother is fully dilated. The shoulders remain impacted. CTG had been reassuring until head-on-perineum sign.

The team is in position around the bed.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 102, BP 134/84 mmHg, RR 20, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.9°C, foetal HR not currently auscultatable
data_table.Examination: Foetal head retracted against perineum, external rotation failed
data_table.Investigations: Cervix fully dilated; foetus presumed cephalic

lead_in: What is the next most appropriate manoeuvre?

options.text: Rubin II internal rotation by pushing the anterior shoulder forward
options.rationale: After McRoberts and suprapubic pressure fail, the next HELPERR step in AU practice is an internal manoeuvre, commencing with Rubin II to reduce shoulder diameter. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia 2020)

options.text: Symphysiotomy under local anaesthetic to widen the pelvic outlet
options.rationale: Symphysiotomy is a last-resort manoeuvre with high maternal morbidity, after all internal manoeuvres and Zavanelli have failed. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia 2020)

options.text: Apply axial traction with stronger maternal pushing efforts
options.rationale: Stronger axial traction risks brachial plexus injury and does not resolve impaction; internal manoeuvres are required. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia 2020)

options.text: Zavanelli manoeuvre with cephalic replacement and immediate caesarean
options.rationale: Zavanelli is reserved for failure of internal manoeuvres; it is the AU last-resort before symphysiotomy. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia 2020)

options.text: Fundal pressure to drive the anterior shoulder under the symphysis
options.rationale: Fundal pressure worsens impaction and is contraindicated in shoulder dystocia. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia 2020)

explanation.summary: Shoulder dystocia is a time-critical emergency. The HELPERR sequence is: call for Help, evaluate for Episiotomy, McRoberts hyperflexion of maternal legs, suprapubic Pressure, then internal manoeuvres (Enter the vagina) including Rubin II and Wood's screw, deliver the posterior arm (Reach), and Roll the patient onto all fours. If all manoeuvres fail, Zavanelli or symphysiotomy are last resorts. Fundal pressure is contraindicated throughout.
key_points: After McRoberts and suprapubic, go internal
Rubin II reduces bisacromial diameter
Fundal pressure is contraindicated
explanation.pearls: Each manoeuvre is given approximately 30 seconds; the head-to-body delivery interval is associated with hypoxic injury when it exceeds 5 minutes.

stem:
A 36-year-old G2P1 at 38 weeks presents with progressive shortness of breath for ten days, two-pillow orthopnoea, and bilateral pitting oedema to the knees. She has no history of cardiac disease. There is no chest pain. Echocardiogram shows global LV hypokinesis with LVEF 28%, no valvular lesion, and a moderate pericardial effusion. NT-proBNP is 5400 ng/L (normal <300). CTG is reassuring. She is currently in spontaneous early labour with the cervix 3 cm dilated, foetus cephalic, no membrane rupture.

Multidisciplinary delivery planning is in progress.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 118, BP 106/64 mmHg, RR 28, SpO2 94%, temperature 36.7°C, foetal HR 140
data_table.Examination: Raised JVP 5 cm, bibasal crackles, bilateral pitting oedema, S3 gallop
data_table.Investigations: LVEF 28%, NT-proBNP 5400 ng/L; CTG reassuring; cervix 3 cm dilated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate delivery plan?

options.text: Assisted vaginal birth under epidural with shortened second stage
options.rationale: In peripartum cardiomyopathy without obstetric indication for caesarean, vaginal birth with epidural and assisted second stage reduces cardiac stress compared with caesarean; multidisciplinary input is essential. (Source: ESC Cardiovascular Disease in Pregnancy 2018)

options.text: Immediate category 1 caesarean section under general anaesthetic now
options.rationale: Category 1 caesarean is for foetal or maternal compromise; haemodynamics are stable and CTG reassuring. (Source: ESC Cardiovascular Disease in Pregnancy 2018)

options.text: Nifedipine tocolysis and elective caesarean section at 40 weeks
options.rationale: Nifedipine reduces preload and may worsen output; prolonging pregnancy in PPCM does not benefit either patient. (Source: ESC Cardiovascular Disease in Pregnancy 2018)

options.text: Bromocriptine 2.5 mg twice daily to delay delivery for optimisation
options.rationale: Bromocriptine is investigational adjunct postpartum; it does not replace delivery in established labour. (Source: ESC Cardiovascular Disease in Pregnancy 2018)

options.text: Insert intra-aortic balloon pump pre-emptively before delivery
options.rationale: Mechanical circulatory support is reserved for cardiogenic shock or severe pulmonary oedema unresponsive to medical therapy. (Source: ESC Cardiovascular Disease in Pregnancy 2018)

explanation.summary: Peripartum cardiomyopathy with LVEF <30% at term is high-risk for decompensation. AU multidisciplinary delivery planning favours vaginal birth with regional anaesthesia in stable patients because caesarean fluid shifts and supine compression worsen failure. Invasive arterial monitoring is used. A shortened second stage with instrumental delivery limits Valsalva. Caesarean is reserved for usual obstetric indications or maternal collapse. Anticoagulation, beta-blockade, and bromocriptine are postpartum considerations.
key_points: PPCM stable: vaginal birth with epidural preferred
Caesarean reserved for obstetric indications
Shortened second stage with instrumental
explanation.pearls: Bromocriptine 2.5 mg twice daily added to standard heart failure therapy postpartum has been associated with improved LV recovery in PPCM in IPAC and BRO-HF trials.

stem:
A 30-year-old G1P0 at 33 weeks presents with right upper quadrant pain and nausea for 18 hours. She has had no previous antenatal hypertension. Examination shows tender liver edge. Urine dipstick 3+ protein. Bloods: haemoglobin 92 g/L (115-165), platelets 40 x10^9/L (150-400), AST 380 U/L (0-40), ALT 410 U/L (0-40), LDH 920 U/L (140-280), creatinine 102 micromol/L (45-90), uric acid 0.48 mmol/L (0.15-0.35), fibrinogen 2.8 g/L. INR 1.0. Blood film shows fragmentation. CTG is reassuring.

The team has been briefed and consent obtained for delivery.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 96, BP 168/108 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.7°C, foetal HR 142
data_table.Investigations: Haemoglobin 92 g/L, platelets 40 x10^9/L, AST 380 U/L, ALT 410 U/L, LDH 920 U/L, creatinine 102 micromol/L, urate 0.48 mmol/L, fibrinogen 2.8 g/L, INR 1.0; film fragmentation

lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan for delivery and anaesthesia?

options.text: Caesarean under GA after labetalol, magnesium, and platelets >50
options.rationale: Platelets <50 x10^9/L contraindicates neuraxial anaesthesia; HELLP at 33 weeks with BP control and seizure prophylaxis warrants delivery under general. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertension in Pregnancy 2023)

options.text: Epidural and assisted vaginal birth after platelet transfusion
options.rationale: Even after transfusion, neuraxial is high-risk in HELLP because of falling platelets and bleeding tendency; vaginal birth has prolonged exposure. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertension in Pregnancy 2023)

options.text: Spinal anaesthesia without platelet transfusion at current platelet count
options.rationale: Spinal at platelet <50 x10^9/L carries unacceptable spinal haematoma risk. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertension in Pregnancy 2023)

options.text: Conservative management with steroids; reassess delivery at 48 h
options.rationale: HELLP at >=34 weeks or with severe maternal complications requires delivery; conservative management beyond steroid window is not standard for severe HELLP. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertension in Pregnancy 2023)

options.text: Induction of labour with oxytocin and IV pethidine for analgesia
options.rationale: Pethidine is contraindicated in HELLP because of seizure threshold and accumulation of norpethidine; induction with severe HELLP risks deterioration during prolonged labour. (Source: AMH: Pethidine)

explanation.summary: HELLP at 33 weeks with platelets 40, AST and ALT >400, and LDH >600 represents Mississippi Class 1 severe disease. Delivery is the only definitive treatment. Severe hypertension is controlled to <160/110 with IV labetalol or hydralazine; magnesium sulfate 4 g IV loading then 1 g/hour prevents eclamptic seizures. Neuraxial anaesthesia requires platelets >=70-80 x10^9/L for epidural and >=50 x10^9/L for spinal per individual unit thresholds, with falling counts a relative contraindication. General anaesthetic is preferred when platelets are <50 and falling.
key_points: Severe HELLP needs delivery, no temporising beyond steroid window
Platelets <50 contraindicate neuraxial when falling
Magnesium sulfate plus IV antihypertensive precede delivery
explanation.pearls: Dexamethasone for HELLP improves laboratory values but not maternal or neonatal outcomes; the Cochrane review found no benefit beyond foetal lung maturity dosing.

subtopic_detail: ECT consent and side effect counselling
stem:
A 58-year-old woman with severe melancholic depression and active suicidal intent has failed two adequate antidepressant trials and one augmentation. She is barely eating and has lost 8 kg in six weeks. The consultant psychiatrist has recommended a course of bilateral electroconvulsive therapy. She is competent to consent. She wishes to understand the most likely adverse effects before signing. She asks specifically about memory.

The registrar is undertaking the consent discussion.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 88, BP 118/76 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 98%, temperature 36.6°C
data_table.Examination: Psychomotor retardation, flattened affect, no neurological deficit
data_table.Investigations: Bloods within normal limits; ECG normal sinus rhythm; CT brain normal
data_table.Medications: Mirtazapine 45 mg, lithium 750 mg/day, lorazepam 1 mg PRN

lead_in: Which adverse effect should be emphasised as the most common and persistent?

options.text: Memory impairment around the treatment period, usually improving in months
options.rationale: Memory disturbance is the most common ECT side effect and the one most relevant to consent; it usually improves but may persist for some events around treatment. (Source: RANZCP ECT Position Statement 2022)

options.text: Permanent persistent global cognitive impairment in over half of patients
options.rationale: Persistent global cognitive impairment is uncommon; baseline cognition typically returns by 6 months. (Source: RANZCP ECT Position Statement 2022)

options.text: Brain damage with structural MRI changes after every treatment session
options.rationale: There is no evidence of structural brain damage from ECT on MRI or histopathology. (Source: RANZCP ECT Position Statement 2022)

options.text: Cardiac arrhythmia with mortality risk above 1 in 100 sessions
options.rationale: ECT mortality is approximately 2 per 100,000 treatments, similar to general anaesthetic alone. (Source: RANZCP ECT Position Statement 2022)

options.text: Seizure threshold rise that always requires switching to ketamine anaesthesia
options.rationale: Seizure threshold does rise over a course but is managed by adjusting stimulus dose; ketamine substitution is for specific resistant cases. (Source: RANZCP ECT Position Statement 2022)

explanation.summary: Modern ECT is performed under general anaesthetic with muscle relaxation, and remains the most effective treatment for severe depression. The most common adverse effect is cognitive: transient confusion immediately post-treatment, then anterograde and retrograde amnesia mostly for events around the treatment period. Most patients recover baseline cognition within weeks to months. Mortality is comparable to general anaesthetic alone (approximately 2 per 100,000 treatments). Headache and muscle ache occur in around 25%.
key_points: Memory effects are the dominant ECT adverse effect
Mortality is comparable to GA alone
Cognitive recovery typically within months
explanation.pearls: Bilateral ECT has greater cognitive impact than right unilateral; many AU services begin with right unilateral and switch only for non-response after 6 treatments.

stem:
A 24-year-old woman is referred to the community mental health team with a two-year pattern of unstable mood, intense fear of abandonment, repeated self-cutting following arguments with her partner, two previous brief admissions following overdoses, and chronic feelings of emptiness. She meets DSM-5 criteria for borderline personality disorder. She has tried sertraline and quetiapine without sustained benefit. She is currently working part time. She has no current intent.

She asks what she should be offered now.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.6°C
data_table.Examination: Multiple healed superficial forearm scars, mood reactive, no current intent
data_table.Investigations: Bloods within normal limits, urine drug screen negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment recommendation?

options.text: Structured DBT or mentalisation-based psychotherapy as primary treatment
options.rationale: AU NHMRC and RANZCP guidelines specify structured psychotherapy as the primary treatment for BPD; medications have limited role and are not first-line. (Source: NHMRC BPD Clinical Practice Guideline)

options.text: Lithium 750 mg/day for affective instability as monotherapy
options.rationale: Lithium does not have an evidence base for BPD as monotherapy; psychotherapy is primary. (Source: NHMRC BPD Clinical Practice Guideline)

options.text: Long-term admission to a tertiary inpatient psychiatric unit
options.rationale: Prolonged inpatient care for BPD is associated with regression and dependence; structured outpatient psychotherapy is preferred. (Source: NHMRC BPD Clinical Practice Guideline)

options.text: Increase quetiapine to 300 mg daily as long-term treatment
options.rationale: Antipsychotics have only short-term and symptom-targeted role in BPD; long-term use is not recommended. (Source: NHMRC BPD Clinical Practice Guideline)

options.text: Cease all pharmacotherapy and offer supportive case management only
options.rationale: Supportive management alone is not sufficient when structured psychotherapy is available; AU guidance specifies psychotherapy as primary. (Source: NHMRC BPD Clinical Practice Guideline)

explanation.summary: Borderline personality disorder is best managed with structured evidence-based psychotherapy. AU NHMRC and RANZCP guidelines list DBT, mentalisation-based therapy, schema-focused therapy, and transference-focused psychotherapy as effective. Pharmacotherapy is not first-line; specific target symptoms (impulsivity, mood instability) may be treated symptomatically with short courses. Brief admissions are appropriate for crisis stabilisation but prolonged admissions are not recommended.
key_points: Structured psychotherapy is primary BPD treatment
Medications are symptom-targeted, not core
Avoid prolonged admissions
explanation.pearls: DBT skills training in groups reduces self-harm and emergency presentations by approximately 50% within 12 months when completed.

stem:
A 28-year-old presents with sudden left-sided weakness after a confrontation at work. Neurological examination shows give-way weakness, Hoover sign positive, and inconsistent power on distraction. MRI brain and cervical cord are normal. The patient appears distressed and concerned about the symptoms. There is no clear external incentive identified. She lives independently and is employed. She has no history of inconsistent presentations across hospitals.

The neurology team has referred for opinion.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 84, BP 116/72 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Give-way weakness left arm and leg, positive Hoover sign, inconsistent power on distraction
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain and cervical cord normal; routine bloods within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Functional neurological disorder (conversion disorder)
options.rationale: Positive Hoover sign and inconsistent neurology with patient distress and no external incentive support a positive diagnosis of functional neurological disorder. (Source: DSM-5-TR; UK Functional Neurological Disorder consensus)
source_refs: DSM-5-TR; UK Functional Neurological Disorder consensus

options.text: Malingering with an external secondary gain motivation
options.rationale: Malingering requires an external incentive (avoiding work, financial gain, legal); none is present here. (Source: DSM-5-TR; UK Functional Neurological Disorder consensus)
source_refs: DSM-5-TR; UK Functional Neurological Disorder consensus

options.text: Factitious disorder with deliberate symptom fabrication for sick role
options.rationale: Factitious disorder requires conscious symptom fabrication for the sick role; clinical pattern here is involuntary distress without fabrication. (Source: DSM-5-TR; UK Functional Neurological Disorder consensus)
source_refs: DSM-5-TR; UK Functional Neurological Disorder consensus

options.text: Acute ischaemic stroke not yet visible on early imaging
options.rationale: Hoover positive and distractibility are not stroke findings; normal MRI within hours of presentation does not exclude stroke but inconsistent power on distraction is functional. (Source: DSM-5-TR; UK Functional Neurological Disorder consensus)
source_refs: DSM-5-TR; UK Functional Neurological Disorder consensus

options.text: Multiple sclerosis presenting with first demyelinating event
options.rationale: MS would not produce Hoover positive findings or distractibility; MRI is normal. (Source: DSM-5-TR; UK Functional Neurological Disorder consensus)
source_refs: DSM-5-TR; UK Functional Neurological Disorder consensus

explanation.summary: Functional neurological disorder is a positive diagnosis based on internal inconsistency of neurological signs (Hoover sign for leg weakness, tremor entrainment, give-way power, distractibility). It is not a diagnosis of exclusion. Patients are typically distressed and concerned. Malingering and factitious disorder differ by conscious symptom production; only malingering has external incentive. Treatment is multidisciplinary including physiotherapy, psychoeducation, and CBT.
key_points: FND is a positive diagnosis using internal inconsistency
Malingering needs external incentive
Factitious is conscious for sick role
explanation.pearls: Naming the diagnosis clearly using positive signs (Hoover) reduces healthcare utilisation more than ambiguous explanations - patients often improve when given the diagnosis and a referral to FND physiotherapy.

sources.label: DSM-5-TR; UK Functional Neurological Disorder consensus

stem:
A 31-year-old woman presents requesting assessment for autism. She reports lifelong difficulties with social interpretation, sensory sensitivities to noise and clothing, intense special interests in marine biology since childhood, and exhaustion after social interactions that she finds requires extensive preparation. She has high academic achievement and is employed as a librarian. She has previously been diagnosed with social anxiety and persistent depressive disorder. She scores 138 on the CAT-Q (camouflaging autistic traits). The GP letter notes apparent eye contact and conventional conversation in the consult.

The psychiatry registrar is reviewing referral options.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76, BP 118/74 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.6°C
data_table.Examination: Mental state with appropriate but rehearsed-feeling social engagement

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer for structured ADOS-2 plus ADI-R assessment by an experienced clinician
options.rationale: AU national autism guideline mandates a structured diagnostic assessment using validated tools by an experienced clinician for adult diagnosis, especially given the masking presentation. (Source: NHMRC National Guideline for Autism Diagnosis 2018)
source_refs: NHMRC National Guideline for Autism Diagnosis 2018

options.text: Reassure that adult diagnosis is not useful and continue treating social anxiety
options.rationale: Adult diagnosis can provide identity, access to NDIS supports, and adjustment of treatment; dismissing the request is not consistent with AU guidance. (Source: NHMRC National Guideline for Autism Diagnosis 2018)
source_refs: NHMRC National Guideline for Autism Diagnosis 2018

options.text: Diagnose ASD based on the AQ-50 and CAT-Q screening results alone
options.rationale: Self-report screens cannot make a diagnosis; structured clinician-administered assessment is required. (Source: NHMRC National Guideline for Autism Diagnosis 2018)
source_refs: NHMRC National Guideline for Autism Diagnosis 2018

options.text: Order genetic chromosomal microarray testing before clinical assessment
options.rationale: Chromosomal microarray is offered after diagnosis when there are features of syndromic autism; it does not precede clinical diagnostic assessment. (Source: NHMRC National Guideline for Autism Diagnosis 2018)
source_refs: NHMRC National Guideline for Autism Diagnosis 2018

options.text: Start risperidone for autism-related symptoms before completing assessment
options.rationale: Risperidone is reserved for severe irritability and aggression in ASD, not core social features; it is never started before diagnosis. (Source: NHMRC National Guideline for Autism Diagnosis 2018)
source_refs: NHMRC National Guideline for Autism Diagnosis 2018

explanation.summary: Adult ASD diagnosis, especially in women, requires a structured assessment to overcome masking and camouflaging. The AU National Guideline recommends use of the ADOS-2 plus a developmental history (ADI-R or 3Di-Adult) by a clinician experienced in adult presentations. Self-report screens (AQ-50, RAADS-R, CAT-Q) are valuable indicators but cannot replace assessment. Diagnosis enables NDIS access and reframes prior misdiagnoses such as social anxiety or BPD.
key_points: Structured ADOS-2 + developmental history is the gold standard
Self-report screens do not diagnose
Female presentation often masks - require experienced clinician
explanation.pearls: Women diagnosed in adulthood have higher rates of co-occurring depression and PTSD than men, and benefit from autism-specific therapy rather than generic CBT.

sources.label: NHMRC National Guideline for Autism Diagnosis 2018

stem:
A 42-year-old man who arrived in Australia from Iraq four months ago presents to the GP with persistent headaches, body aches, palpitations, and disturbed sleep. He fled torture and incarceration in 2021. Through an interpreter, he says his head feels heavy and his chest feels tight. He cannot return to his country. He is supported by an asylum support service. He denies depressed mood when asked directly but reports nightmares and avoiding crowds. He is on a temporary protection visa.

A family member is in the room.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 88, BP 128/78 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: No focal neurology, cardiovascular and respiratory examinations within normal limits
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs, TSH, B12, ferritin, vitamin D within normal limits; ECG sinus rhythm

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Professional interpreter (excluding family) and refugee trauma service referral
options.rationale: Refugee somatic complaints often mask PTSD; trauma history requires a professional interpreter without family present, and AU refugee trauma services (e.g. STARTTS, Foundation House) are the appropriate referral pathway. (Source: ASID and Australian Refugee Health Practice Guide)

options.text: Reassure normal investigations and discharge with no further follow-up
options.rationale: Somatic complaints in refugees often signal underlying PTSD; reassurance alone misses the diagnosis. (Source: Australian Refugee Health Practice Guide)

options.text: Use the family member as interpreter to discuss trauma history
options.rationale: Family interpreters compromise confidentiality, miss disclosure, and create harm; a professional interpreter is mandated. (Source: Australian Refugee Health Practice Guide)

options.text: Prescribe diazepam 5 mg three times daily for ongoing anxiety
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines worsen PTSD outcomes and carry dependence risk; they are not first-line. (Source: Australian Refugee Health Practice Guide)

options.text: Refer to general adult mental health service without trauma-informed expertise
options.rationale: Generic services may lack interpreters and trauma-specific therapies; specialist refugee services are the correct AU pathway. (Source: Australian Refugee Health Practice Guide)

explanation.summary: Refugees presenting with somatic complaints commonly have underlying PTSD, depression, or anxiety. Confidential history-taking with a professional (preferably gender-matched) interpreter is essential; family interpreters miss disclosure and may pose safety risks. Australian specialist refugee trauma services (STARTTS NSW, Foundation House VIC, ASeTTS WA, QPASTT QLD) provide trauma-focused therapy with culturally appropriate care. Benzodiazepines worsen PTSD outcomes and are not first-line.
key_points: Refugee somatic complaints can mask PTSD
Professional interpreter required, not family
Refer to specialist refugee trauma service
explanation.pearls: Refugees on temporary protection visas have restricted Medicare and may have no access to PBS-subsidised medications; check eligibility before prescribing.

stem:
A 27-year-old man with schizophrenia, currently on a community treatment order, missed two depot appointments in the past month. His mother phones the community mental health team to report he is increasingly suspicious, not eating well, and has stopped his oral medications. He lives with his parents. He has not contacted the service. He has a history of two prior involuntary admissions following relapse during medication non-adherence. He has not been violent.

The registrar is determining the next clinical step.

data_table.Vital signs: Last recorded 6 weeks ago: pulse 78, BP 124/78 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.6°C
data_table.Examination: Last MSE 6 weeks ago: stable, well-engaged
data_table.Investigations: Last clozapine level 6 weeks ago therapeutic
data_table.Medications: Paliperidone depot monthly (last dose 5 weeks ago)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first action?

options.text: Urgent home visit (with police if safety risks) to assess under the CTO
options.rationale: CTOs in AU jurisdictions authorise community follow-up and assessment; the first step in suspected relapse is direct clinical assessment, with police only if safety risks. (Source: NSW Mental Health Act 2007)

options.text: Apply immediately for an inpatient involuntary treatment order without further assessment
options.rationale: An inpatient order requires direct assessment of mental state and risk; second-hand report from family does not substitute. (Source: NSW Mental Health Act 2007)

options.text: Reassure the mother and wait until the next scheduled depot appointment in three weeks
options.rationale: Waiting risks deterioration; the relapse pattern of medication non-adherence followed by admission is well-documented for this patient. (Source: NSW Mental Health Act 2007)

options.text: Discharge the patient from the CTO since he has not contacted the service
options.rationale: Non-contact is a sign for follow-up, not for discharge; revoking a CTO without assessment leaves no legal framework for community care. (Source: NSW Mental Health Act 2007)

options.text: Issue a breach notice under the CTO with no further clinical action this week
options.rationale: Breach administration without clinical assessment is not appropriate; the CTO authorises hands-on review. (Source: NSW Mental Health Act 2007)

explanation.summary: Community treatment orders authorise mental health services to follow up, assess, and enforce treatment in the community for patients with severe mental illness who would otherwise relapse. When a carer reports concern, the AU response is direct clinical assessment, usually by home visit with appropriate safety planning. Police may attend if there is risk of harm. Involuntary admission requires mental state examination demonstrating the criteria. Family information is taken seriously but does not replace clinical assessment.
key_points: CTO authorises home visit assessment
Police only if safety risks
Family report is a trigger to assess, not to admit
explanation.pearls: Most AU jurisdictions require a clinical review within 2-3 working days of CTO breach to either restore adherence or escalate to inpatient assessment; document the timeline in clinical notes.

sources.label: RANZCP Mental Health Legislation Position Statement

stem:
A 26-year-old man brought in by police is shouting, threatening staff, and pacing in the assessment room. He has known schizophrenia and has had two previous similar presentations. De-escalation by an experienced clinician and offer of oral medication have failed. He has been started on a section under the Mental Health Act. He has no known cardiac history. He has not received any medication tonight. Body weight is approximately 80 kg. He is medication-naive in the last 24 hours.

The team has the safe area set up and four trained staff present.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 118, BP 148/92 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 98%, temperature 37.0°C
data_table.Examination: Hyperaroused, pacing, shouting, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: ECG and bloods not yet obtainable

lead_in: What is the most appropriate intramuscular regimen?

options.text: Olanzapine 10 mg IM as a single dose, repeated once at 30 minutes
options.rationale: AU acute behavioural disturbance guidelines list IM olanzapine 10 mg as first-line monotherapy when ECG is not available and oral medication declined; midazolam adds respiratory risk. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Acute Behavioural Disturbance)

options.text: Olanzapine 10 mg IM plus midazolam 10 mg IM simultaneously
options.rationale: Combining IM olanzapine with parenteral benzodiazepines is contraindicated by the manufacturer because of risk of cardiorespiratory depression. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Acute Behavioural Disturbance)

options.text: Droperidol 10 mg IM combined with IM midazolam 5 mg as primary regimen
options.rationale: Droperidol monotherapy is an alternative but routine combination with midazolam is reserved for refractory cases; ECG cannot be obtained safely. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Acute Behavioural Disturbance)

options.text: Haloperidol 5 mg IM combined with promethazine 25 mg IM dose
options.rationale: This is the UK NICE preferred combination but in AU droperidol or olanzapine is preferred because of haloperidol QT and EPSE risks. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Acute Behavioural Disturbance)

options.text: Ketamine 200 mg IM as first-line chemical restraint dose
options.rationale: Ketamine is reserved for pre-hospital agitated delirium under retrieval physician oversight, not first-line for ED behavioural emergency. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Acute Behavioural Disturbance)

explanation.summary: Australian acute behavioural disturbance guidelines (eTG and most state mental health pathways) list IM droperidol 10 mg or IM olanzapine 10 mg as first-line single-agent rapid tranquillisation when oral medication has been declined. IM olanzapine and parenteral benzodiazepines should not be combined because of respiratory depression. Continuous monitoring with pulse oximetry is required. ECG is performed when feasible to detect QT prolongation. Reassessment at 30 minutes guides repeat dosing.
key_points: AU first-line: IM olanzapine 10 mg or IM droperidol 10 mg
Do not combine IM olanzapine with parenteral benzodiazepines
Continuous monitoring including SpO2
explanation.pearls: Patients with intoxication, hypovolaemia, or significant medical illness need lower starting doses (e.g. 5 mg olanzapine) and individual airway plans; the standard 10 mg is for a fit adult.

sources.label: RANZCP Acute Behavioural Disturbance Position Statement
